notes: West Africa expansion wave covering underrepresented Beninese, Burkinabe, Cape Verdean, Gambian, Ghanaian, Ivorian, Liberian, Malian, Mauritanian, Nigerien, Sierra Leonean, and Togolese dishes. Recipes are synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references, not copied from a single source.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Atassi Watche
recipes.description: Beninese rice and beans cooked tender and served with a concentrated tomato dja sauce, eggs, fish, or wagasi.
recipes.story: Atassi in southern Benin and Watche in the north is one of the country's everyday anchors: rice and beans cooked together, then lifted by dja, pepper, fish, egg, cheese, or meat.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch plates, market-style meals, and meal prep.
recipes.best_served_with: Dja tomato sauce, fried fish, boiled eggs, wagasi cheese, or chili.
recipes.regional_variations: Northern versions may use potash and a firmer bean texture, while southern plates often lean into oily tomato dja and fried accompaniments.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree that the core is rice and beans, commonly served with dja or friture, plus optional fish, eggs, cheese, or meat. The AfroKitchen version keeps the grain ratio balanced and gives the sauce its own simmer.
recommended_changes: Soak the beans so they cook before the rice breaks down.
Salt after the beans soften, not at the beginning.

sources.title: Embassy Direct - Atassi or Watche Benin
Benin News - Atassi ou Watche ou Ayimolou

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in water until just tender but not falling apart.

steps.title: Add the rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rinsed rice and salt, then cook uncovered until the rice is tender and the beans hold their shape.

steps.title: Build the dja
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add tomatoes, tomato paste, dried shrimp, ginger, and garlic, then simmer into a thick red sauce.

steps.title: Serve market-style
steps.instruction: Spoon rice and beans onto plates and top with dja, fish, egg, wagasi, or chili.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Poisson au Moyo
recipes.description: Beninese grilled fish with a bright raw-style tomato, onion, lemon, herb, and chili moyo sauce.
recipes.story: Moyo is the sauce that makes simple grilled fish feel like a full coastal plate: tomato, onion, chile, lemon, herbs, and enough salt to wake up the fish.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend grills, beach-style lunches, and quick seafood dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Atassi, ablo, akassa, fried plantain, or steamed rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks keep moyo raw and sharp, while others briefly warm the tomato-onion mixture with oil or smoked fish.
recipes.review_summary: Sources point to grilled or braised fish served with a fresh tomato-onion-chili moyo. The AfroKitchen version seasons the fish simply and keeps the sauce bright.
recommended_changes: Use firm fish so it does not break on the grill.
Salt the sauce shortly before serving so the tomatoes stay lively.

sources.title: I've Been Cooking - Poisson Braise au Moyo

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish with oil, paprika, salt, and pepper, including the cuts along the sides.

steps.title: Make the moyo
steps.instruction: Mix tomatoes, onion, chilies, lemon juice, herbs, oil, and salt. Let it stand while the fish cooks.

steps.title: Grill the fish
steps.instruction: Grill or broil the fish until the skin chars lightly and the flesh flakes cleanly.

steps.title: Spoon and serve
steps.instruction: Top the fish with moyo and serve immediately while the sauce is fresh.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Dakouin
recipes.description: Mono-region Beninese fish, tomato, and gari dish where cassava granules thicken a deeply seasoned fish broth.
recipes.story: Dakouin is practical coastal cooking: fresh fish becomes broth, tomatoes give body, and gari turns the pot into a spoonable cassava meal.
recipes.occasion: Best for fresh-fish days, family lunch, and rainy-season comfort.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra moyo, sliced onion, or a small pepper sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use tilapia, others use the day's catch, and the gari can be loose like couscous or thick like a soft mash.
recipes.review_summary: Sources identify Dakouin as fish, tomatoes, and gari. The AfroKitchen version makes a separate seasoned broth before folding in gari so the texture stays controlled.
recommended_changes: Add gari gradually; it thickens fast.
Keep the fish pieces large so they survive the final fold.

sources.title: Recettes d'Afrique - Dakouin avec sauce rouge

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes, onion, garlic, pepper, oil, and water until the tomatoes collapse.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add fish pieces and simmer gently until cooked through.

steps.title: Fold in gari
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and sprinkle in gari gradually, stirring until the broth thickens but stays moist.
steps.tip: Stop before it becomes stiff; it firms as it rests.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Cover for a few minutes, adjust salt, and serve hot with extra sauce.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Benga
recipes.description: Burkinabe rice and beans plate, simple, nourishing, and usually finished with tomato-onion sauce or chili oil.
recipes.story: Benga means beans in Moore and the dish carries that simplicity proudly: beans, rice, sauce, pepper, and a pot that can feed a household.
recipes.occasion: Best for everyday lunch, school-night cooking, and market plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, chili oil, fried onions, or grilled chicken.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households cook rice and beans together, while others serve tender beans over rice with sauce.
recipes.review_summary: Sources frame benga as a central Burkinabe beans dish, commonly paired with rice and sauce. The AfroKitchen version cooks beans first, then folds in rice for a reliable texture.
recommended_changes: Use soaked beans for even cooking.
Keep the sauce separate if serving to a crowd.

sources.title: Infos Culture du Faso - Le benga

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in water until tender.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and enough hot water to cover. Cook until the rice is tender and liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Make sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add tomatoes, tomato paste, pepper, and salt, then simmer into a spoonable sauce.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Serve rice and beans with the sauce spooned over the top.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Gonre
recipes.description: Burkinabe steamed bean cakes made from cowpea paste, peanut, leafy greens, and maize couscous.
recipes.story: Gonre is a Mossi bean dish with two moods: smooth in the dry season and leaf-rich when bean leaves are available.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast markets, snack plates, and ceremonial family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, chili oil, or a light onion relish.
recipes.regional_variations: Leafy versions include bean leaves or greens; simpler versions are mostly cowpea paste and seasoning.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree that gonre is based on cowpeas and often steamed into cakes, sometimes enriched with greens, peanuts, maize couscous, and potash. The AfroKitchen version skips potash unless available.
recommended_changes: Whip the bean paste so the cakes are not heavy.
Oil the steamer leaves or cups before filling.

ingredients.name: finely chopped bean leaves or spinach

steps.title: Blend the beans
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with onion, garlic, chili, and just enough water to make a thick paste.

steps.title: Season and rest
steps.instruction: Fold in peanut powder, greens, maize couscous, oil, and salt. Let the mixture hydrate.

steps.title: Steam cakes
steps.instruction: Spoon into oiled cups or leaves and steam until firm through the center.

steps.title: Serve warm
steps.instruction: Unmold and serve with tomato sauce or chili oil.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Zamne
recipes.description: Burkinabe Acacia seed dish simmered until tender, then dressed with onion, tomato, oil, and chile.
recipes.story: Zamne turns a wild Sahelian seed into a celebration food: long-cooked, chewy, nutty, and often treated like a prized local ingredient.
recipes.occasion: Best for special meals, ceremonies, and vegetarian sharing plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, couscous, grilled meat, or a tomato-onion relish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks serve zamne as a salad, some simmer it with meat, and some dress it simply with oil, onion, tomato, and pepper.
recipes.review_summary: FAO and food science references confirm zamne as an Acacia macrostachya seed dish from Burkina Faso. The AfroKitchen method uses a long boil and a simple tomato-onion finish.
recommended_changes: Soak and rinse the seeds well.
Cook until tender before adding acidic tomato.

sources.title: PMC - Physical, cooking, and nutritional properties of Zamne

steps.title: Boil the seeds
steps.instruction: Rinse soaked zamne and boil in plenty of water until tender.

steps.title: Drain and season
steps.instruction: Drain the seeds and salt them lightly while hot.

steps.title: Cook relish
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil until soft, then add tomato, pepper, and soumbala until jammy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold zamne through the relish and simmer briefly so the seeds absorb the sauce.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Jagacida
recipes.description: Cape Verdean beans and rice cooked with onion, garlic, bay, and linguiça-style sausage or kept vegetarian.
recipes.story: Jagacida, often shortened to jag, is the kind of Cape Verdean rice dish that can be a side, a quick lunch, or the base for a bigger plate.
recipes.occasion: Best for weeknight dinners, cookouts, and family sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Grilled fish, cachupa leftovers, fried eggs, or hot sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some families use kidney beans, lima beans, or peas; some add linguiça while others keep it vegetarian.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on a Cape Verdean beans-and-rice base with onion and garlic, sometimes with sausage. The AfroKitchen version treats sausage as optional.
recommended_changes: Use cooked beans or canned beans so the rice timing stays predictable.
Let the rice steam off heat before fluffing.

sources.title: Oldways - Jagacida Cape Verdean Beans Rice

ingredients.name: cooked kidney beans or lima beans

steps.title: Brown aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and sausage in oil until fragrant and lightly browned.

steps.title: Coat the rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rice, beans, bay leaves, salt, and pepper so the grains are glossy.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water or stock, cover, and cook until rice is tender.

steps.title: Steam and fluff
steps.instruction: Rest off heat, covered, then fluff gently and serve.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Xerem
recipes.description: Cape Verdean cracked-corn porridge or stew, cooked savory with bay, butter, beans, greens, and smoked meat or fish.
recipes.story: Xerem shows Cape Verde's corn table from another angle: not cachupa, but a thick cracked-corn dish that can be simple or feast-day rich.
recipes.occasion: Best for cool evenings, family pots, and rustic one-bowl meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Braised greens, fish stew, beans, or hot sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are plain and buttery, while feast versions include pork, beans, squash, cabbage, or greens.
recipes.review_summary: Cape Verdean sources identify xerem as a corn dish, often savory and served with soup or enriched as a stew. The AfroKitchen version builds a complete savory pot.
recommended_changes: Stir often because cracked corn catches on the bottom.
Add extra hot water as needed for a soft porridge texture.

sources.title: Travel Food Atlas - Cape Verde Food

steps.title: Start the pot
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and smoked meat in butter or oil until aromatic.

steps.title: Simmer corn
steps.instruction: Add cracked corn, bay leaves, and stock. Simmer, stirring often, until the corn softens.

steps.title: Add beans and greens
steps.instruction: Stir in beans and greens and continue cooking until the pot is thick and cohesive.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Season, rest covered, and serve warm.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Buzio Caboverdiano
recipes.description: Cape Verdean sea snail or conch stew slow-cooked with onion, garlic, tomato, pepper, bay, potatoes, and herbs.
recipes.story: Buzio brings Cape Verde's coastline to the pot: a slow seafood stew where tough conch turns tender and the sauce gathers tomato, bay, and island herbs.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood weekends, coastal menus, and celebration lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, boiled root vegetables, bread, or xerem.
recipes.regional_variations: Depending on the island, cooks may use conch, whelk, mixed shellfish, potatoes, carrots, wine, or soy sauce.
recipes.review_summary: Cape Verdean recipe sources agree that buzio is a slow-cooked shellfish stew. The AfroKitchen version tenderizes conch first, then finishes it in tomato and aromatics.
recommended_changes: Tenderize conch before stewing.
Keep the final simmer gentle so shellfish stays pleasant.

steps.title: Tenderize shellfish
steps.instruction: Simmer or pressure-cook conch in salted water until it begins to soften.

steps.title: Build sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, tomato, pepper, and bay in oil until the tomato breaks down.

steps.title: Stew
steps.instruction: Add conch, potatoes, carrots, and stock. Simmer until vegetables and shellfish are tender.

steps.title: Finish herbs
steps.instruction: Adjust salt, add herbs, and rest briefly before serving.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Plasas
recipes.description: Gambian leafy stew with spinach or cassava leaves, peanut, palm oil, smoked fish, and chili.
recipes.story: Plasas is Gambian leaf stew comfort: greens cooked down with peanut richness, smoked fish depth, and enough pepper to wake up rice or fufu.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice plates, family lunch, and greens-heavy dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, boiled cassava, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Cassava leaves, spinach, bitter leaves, fish, chicken, beef, or vegetarian peanut versions all appear across households.
recipes.review_summary: Gambian references describe plasas as a leafy stew, often spinach or cassava leaves. The AfroKitchen version uses smoked fish and peanut for a common household profile.
recommended_changes: Cook cassava leaves thoroughly if using them.
Add peanut paste after greens soften so it does not scorch.

steps.title: Soften aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and pepper in palm oil until fragrant.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add greens and stock, then simmer until tender and reduced.

steps.title: Add peanut and fish
steps.instruction: Stir in peanut butter and smoked fish. Simmer until thick and rich.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning and serve hot over rice or with fufu.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Gambian Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Gambian chicken marinated in mustard, vinegar or lemon, onion, garlic, pepper, then fried and simmered with onion sauce.
recipes.story: Yassa belongs to the Senegambian table, and in The Gambia it is a trusted chicken-and-rice plate built on sharp onions, mustard, and a good fry before the simmer.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday rice, gatherings, and onion lovers.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, cucumber salad, or fried plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use vinegar, some use lemon, some add carrots or green pepper, and fish yassa is also common.
recipes.review_summary: Gambian sources agree on chicken, onions, mustard, vinegar or lemon, garlic, pepper, frying, and a rice service. The AfroKitchen version gives the onion enough time to soften.
recommended_changes: Marinate at least 30 minutes, longer if possible.
Brown the chicken before simmering for better depth.

sources.title: My Gambia - Traditional Dish Recipe Yassa

steps.title: Marinate chicken
steps.instruction: Mix chicken with onions, mustard, lemon or vinegar, garlic, ginger, pepper, salt, and black pepper.

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Lift chicken from the onions and fry until golden on both sides.

steps.title: Cook onion sauce
steps.instruction: Cook the marinated onions in the same pot until soft and lightly caramelized.

steps.title: Simmer together
steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot with stock and simmer until tender and coated in onion sauce.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Nyambeh Nyebeh
recipes.description: Gambian cassava and beans served with a spicy onion-pepper stew, often eaten for breakfast or dinner.
recipes.story: Nyambeh means cassava and nyebeh means beans, and that tells the truth of the plate: humble roots and legumes made generous with stew.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast plates, dinner bowls, and street-food style meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried fish, onion gravy, pepper sauce, or roasted sweet potato.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks boil cassava and beans separately, some add sweet potato or plantain, and some finish the stew with fish.
recipes.review_summary: Gambian sources agree on cassava, beans, and a separate vegetable stew or gravy. The AfroKitchen version keeps the root and bean textures separate until plating.
recommended_changes: Cook cassava and beans separately so neither turns mushy.
Keep the onion stew bold because the base is mild.

steps.title: Boil beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans until tender, then drain and season lightly.

steps.title: Boil cassava
steps.instruction: Boil cassava in salted water until tender but still holding shape.

steps.title: Make stew
steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, tomato paste, tomatoes, green pepper, and hot pepper into a thick sauce.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Serve cassava and beans with the spicy stew spooned generously over the top.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Tuo Zaafi with Ayoyo Soup
recipes.description: Northern Ghanaian maize swallow served with slippery ayoyo soup and a tomato-pepper stew.
recipes.story: Tuo Zaafi is northern Ghana on a plate: smooth maize dough, ayoyo's draw, and a stew that carries meat, pepper, and dawadawa depth.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch, northern Ghanaian family meals, and soup-and-swallow nights.
recipes.best_served_with: Ayoyo soup, tomato stew, goat, beef, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use maize flour only, some add cassava flour, and ayoyo may be cooked with dawadawa, okra, or local potash.
recipes.review_summary: Sources identify Tuo Zaafi as a maize swallow paired with ayoyo soup and stew. The AfroKitchen version pairs a smooth maize base with jute leaf soup and a simple meat stew.
recommended_changes: Whisk flour into cool water first to prevent lumps.
Beat the dough firmly until smooth and elastic.

sources.title: Pulse Ghana - Tuo Zaafi and Ayoyo Soup

steps.title: Cook stew
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with onion, tomato, pepper, oil, and salt until tender and saucy.

steps.title: Cook ayoyo
steps.instruction: Simmer ayoyo with dawadawa, pepper, and a little water until slippery and bright green.

steps.title: Make tuo
steps.instruction: Whisk maize flour into water, cook to a porridge, then beat in cassava flour until smooth and stretchy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape Tuo Zaafi into mounds and serve with ayoyo soup and stew.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Omo Tuo
recipes.description: Ghanaian rice balls cooked very soft, beaten smooth, and served with groundnut soup or palm nut soup.
recipes.story: Omo Tuo is simple in ingredients but serious in texture: soft rice beaten until smooth enough to roll into balls that drink soup properly.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday groundnut soup, palm nut soup, and family lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Nkate nkwan, palm nut soup, chicken, goat, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use broken rice, some use long-grain rice cooked with extra water, and ball size changes by household.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree that Omo Tuo is rice cooked with extra water, mashed, and shaped into balls, usually paired with groundnut or palm nut soup.
recommended_changes: Use short-grain, broken, or starchy rice for easy shaping.
Wet hands or a bowl before shaping the rice balls.

steps.title: Cook rice soft
steps.instruction: Boil rice with extra water until very soft and slightly overcooked.

steps.title: Beat rice
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and beat the rice firmly with a wooden spoon until sticky and smooth.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Use wet hands or a wet bowl to shape the rice into smooth balls.

steps.title: Serve with soup
steps.instruction: Serve hot with groundnut soup or palm nut soup.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Ga Kenkey with Fried Fish
recipes.description: Ga-style fermented corn dumplings served with fried fish, pepper sauce, and fresh tomato-onion relish.
recipes.story: Ga kenkey is Accra street-food royalty: fermented corn dough, steamed tight, then eaten with fish, pepper, and fresh relish.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend street-food plates, fish lunches, and coastal Ghana menus.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried fish, shito, fresh pepper sauce, tomato, onion, and avocado.
recipes.regional_variations: Ga kenkey is usually salted and wrapped, while Fante kenkey is often less salty and wrapped differently.
recipes.review_summary: Sources describe kenkey as fermented corn dough, often served with fried fish and pepper. The AfroKitchen version assumes prepared fermented dough for safety and practicality.
recommended_changes: Use trusted fermented corn dough if making it at home.
Steam until the center is fully firm.

sources.title: Pulse Ghana - Ga Kenkey with Sauce and Fried Fish

steps.title: Cook a dough starter
steps.instruction: Cook one-third of the corn dough with water into a thick porridge, then mix it back into the raw dough with salt.

steps.title: Wrap and steam
steps.instruction: Shape dough portions, wrap in corn husks or foil, and steam until firm.

steps.title: Fry fish
steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and fry until crisp outside and cooked through.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve kenkey warm with fried fish, pepper sauce, tomato, and onion.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Fufu with Light Soup
recipes.description: Ghanaian cassava-plantain fufu served with peppery tomato-based light soup and goat, chicken, or fish.
recipes.story: Fufu and light soup is a Ghanaian classic built on contrast: elastic swallow, thin peppery soup, and deeply simmered meat or fish.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, celebratory family meals, and cold-weather soup days.
recipes.best_served_with: Goat light soup, chicken light soup, or fish light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Fufu may use cassava with plantain, yam, or cocoyam, and light soup can be goat, chicken, fish, or mixed meat.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on fufu as a pounded cassava/plantain swallow and light soup as a peppery tomato-based broth. The AfroKitchen version keeps the soup light, not stew-thick.
recommended_changes: Do not over-reduce light soup.
Use ripe-but-firm plantain for balanced fufu texture.

sources.title: My Planet Food - Fufu with Light Soup

steps.title: Simmer meat
steps.instruction: Cook meat with onion, ginger, garlic, salt, and water until it begins to soften.

steps.title: Blend soup base
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes and peppers in the pot, lift them out, blend with onion, then return to the soup.

steps.title: Finish light soup
steps.instruction: Continue simmering until meat is tender and the broth is peppery but still light.

steps.title: Make fufu
steps.instruction: Boil cassava and plantain until tender, then pound or process into a smooth elastic mound.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Foutou Sauce Graine
recipes.description: Ivorian pounded plantain or yam foutou served with rich palm nut sauce, smoked meat, fish, and chile.
recipes.story: Foutou sauce graine is one of the plates that makes an Ivorian table feel complete: elastic pounded starch and a red palm sauce with smoke, chile, and depth.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, maquis-style meals, and special family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Foutou banane, foutou igname, placali, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: The sauce may include smoked beef, agouti, fish, crab, adjovan, or green chilies depending on place and budget.
recipes.review_summary: Ivorian sources agree on palm nut juice, smoked protein, tomato, onion, chile, and foutou pairing. The AfroKitchen version uses canned palm base as an accessible option.
recommended_changes: Use real palm nut base when possible.
Simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens and oil beads rise.

sources.title: Cuisine de Chez Nous - Foutou Sauce Graine

ingredients.name: palm nut juice or diluted palm cream

steps.title: Cook protein
steps.instruction: Simmer smoked meat with onion, tomato, pepper, and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Add palm base
steps.instruction: Add palm nut juice and smoked fish, then simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Make foutou
steps.instruction: Boil plantain and yam until tender, then pound together into a smooth elastic dough.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve foutou with hot sauce graine and extra pepper on the side.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Garba
recipes.description: Abidjan street-food plate of attieke, fried tuna, raw onion-tomato-pepper relish, oil, and seasoning.
recipes.story: Garba is Abidjan speed, attitude, and satisfaction: sour cassava couscous, fried tuna, fresh pepper, onion, and that unmistakable street-food oil gloss.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food nights, fast lunches, and game-day plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra chili, sliced onion, and chilled bissap.
recipes.regional_variations: Some vendors dust tuna with flour, some keep it plain, and the attieke may be heavily oiled and seasoned or kept lighter.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on attieke with fried tuna plus onion, tomato, pepper, oil, and seasoning. The AfroKitchen version uses a light flour dusting for crisp tuna.
recommended_changes: Steam attieke instead of microwaving if possible.
Use firm tuna steaks and do not overcook them.

sources.title: Africa Cuisine - Garba Ivorian Fried Tuna

steps.title: Steam attieke
steps.instruction: Sprinkle attieke with water and steam until fluffy, then toss with a little oil and salt.

steps.title: Season tuna
steps.instruction: Salt tuna, dust lightly with flour, and shake off excess.

steps.title: Fry tuna
steps.instruction: Fry tuna until golden outside and just cooked through.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Serve attieke with tuna, onion, tomato, chili, oil, and seasoning.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Placali Sauce Kope
recipes.description: Ivorian fermented cassava placali served with slippery okra sauce, crab, smoked fish, meat, and chile.
recipes.story: Placali and sauce kope is texture food at its finest: sour-soft cassava paste meeting okra's draw and the deep savor of crab, smoked fish, and meat.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend Ivorian tables and serious soup-and-swallow plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra chili, smoked fish, crab, or kplo if available.
recipes.regional_variations: Sauce kope may include crab, snails, beef feet, smoked fish, or only fish; placali can be bought ready-made or cooked from fermented cassava dough.
recipes.review_summary: Ivorian sources confirm placali as fermented cassava paste and sauce kope as an okra-based sauce often served with seafood and meat. The AfroKitchen version uses grated okra for reliable draw.
recommended_changes: Stir placali constantly while it thickens.
Add okra late and avoid over-whisking after the draw forms.

sources.title: Cuisine de Chez Nous - Placali a la sauce kope
Cordon Bleu CI - Kope sauce gombos rapes

steps.title: Cook proteins
steps.instruction: Simmer meat or seafood with onion, tomato, pepper, and stock until tender.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Stir in grated okra and palm oil, then simmer until the sauce becomes glossy and lightly stretchy.

steps.title: Cook placali
steps.instruction: Whisk cassava dough with water over medium heat, stirring constantly until translucent and elastic.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Shape placali and serve with sauce kope spooned around it.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Dumboy
recipes.description: Liberian fresh cassava swallow boiled, pounded smooth, and served with pepper soup, palm butter, or palava sauce.
recipes.story: Dumboy is Liberia's fresh cassava answer to swallow: boiled, pounded, glossy, and ready for any serious soup.
recipes.occasion: Best for pepper soup, palm butter soup, and weekend Liberian meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Pepper soup, palm butter soup, palava sauce, or okra stew.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households pound by hand, others use modern processors, and texture ranges from soft to very elastic.
recipes.review_summary: Liberian references agree that dumboy uses fresh boiled cassava, unlike fermented cassava fufu. The AfroKitchen version focuses on a smooth pounded finish.
recommended_changes: Remove cassava fibers before pounding.
Serve immediately while soft and elastic.

steps.title: Boil cassava
steps.instruction: Boil cassava in water until fully tender.

steps.title: Remove fibers
steps.instruction: Drain, remove woody centers, and keep cassava warm.

steps.title: Pound smooth
steps.instruction: Pound or process hot cassava until smooth, elastic, and cohesive.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Shape into mounds and serve with soup or palm oil.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Liberian Palava Sauce
recipes.description: Liberian leafy stew of greens, okra, chicken, peanut, tomato, and pepper, served with rice or dumboy.
recipes.story: Liberian palava sauce is a leafy stew that knows how to be both practical and lush: greens, okra, pepper, chicken, and peanut body.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice plates, dumboy meals, and family stews.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, dumboy, fufu, or boiled plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Greens can be spinach, collards, cassava leaves, or jute; protein can be chicken, smoked fish, beef, or none.
recipes.review_summary: Sources describe Liberian palava as a green stew with chicken and often peanut or okra. The AfroKitchen version keeps peanut moderate and lets greens lead.
recommended_changes: Simmer greens until tender but not dull.
Whisk peanut butter with broth before adding.

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Season and brown chicken in oil, then remove briefly.

steps.title: Build sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes until softened.

steps.title: Simmer stew
steps.instruction: Return chicken with stock and simmer until nearly tender.

steps.title: Finish greens
steps.instruction: Add greens, okra, and loosened peanut butter. Simmer until thick and seasoned.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: GB Soup
recipes.description: Liberian pepper soup for dumboy, usually built with meat or fish, okra, bitter balls, onion, tomato, and hot pepper.
recipes.story: GB soup is the partner that makes dumboy sing: peppery broth, meat or fish, okra body, and a clean heat that cuts through cassava.
recipes.occasion: Best for dumboy meals, rainy days, and pepper soup cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Dumboy, fufu, boiled cassava, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Goat, chicken, beef, smoked fish, shrimp, okra, and bitter balls can all appear depending on household and county.
recipes.review_summary: Liberian sources connect dumboy with hot pepper soup made with meat, fish, okra, onion, tomato, and bitter balls. The AfroKitchen version keeps the broth clear and pepper-forward.
recommended_changes: Do not thicken it like stew; this should stay brothy.
Cook okra near the end for body without losing freshness.

steps.title: Start broth
steps.instruction: Simmer protein with onion, tomato, pepper, garlic, ginger, salt, and water.

steps.title: Skim and soften
steps.instruction: Skim the broth and continue cooking until the protein is tender.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add okra and bitter balls or eggplant and simmer until just tender.

steps.title: Serve brothy
steps.instruction: Adjust pepper and salt, then serve with dumboy.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Djabadji
recipes.description: Malian onion-rich red sauce with meat, tomato, garlic, and chile, served over rice or with toh.
recipes.story: Djabadji, also called nandji in some references, is the kind of Malian sauce where onions do the heavy lifting and tomato gives the pot its red body.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice bowls, family lunch, and sauce rotation.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, toh, or boiled plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use beef, some chicken, and some add vegetables like carrot, cabbage, or eggplant.
recipes.review_summary: Available Malian recipe sources describe Djabadji or Nandji as a red onion sauce with meat. The AfroKitchen version favors slow onion cooking and moderate tomato.
recommended_changes: Cook onions down patiently before adding much liquid.
Serve with plain rice so the sauce remains the focus.

steps.title: Brown protein
steps.instruction: Season and brown the meat or chicken in oil.

steps.title: Cook onions
steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until soft, sweet, and reduced.

steps.title: Simmer sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, pepper, and stock. Simmer until protein is tender.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve over rice or with toh.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Djouka de Fonio
recipes.description: Malian steamed fonio mixed with roasted peanut powder, okra, onion, and a peppery vegetable garnish.
recipes.story: Djouka is fonio with substance: steamed grains, peanut richness, okra, and a garnish that turns an ancient grain into a full meal.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunches, fasting tables, and grain-forward menus.
recipes.best_served_with: Eggplant-onion garnish, tomato sauce, grilled fish, or roasted vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Mali and Burkina Faso both claim versions; some use potash, some add gombo, and some serve it with a separate sauce.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on fonio mixed with peanut, often okra and a vegetable garnish. The AfroKitchen version steams in stages for fluffy grains.
recommended_changes: Wash fonio carefully to remove grit.
Steam rather than boil for a couscous-like texture.

steps.title: Steam fonio
steps.instruction: Moisten fonio lightly and steam until the grains begin to soften.

steps.title: Add peanut and okra
steps.instruction: Work in peanut powder, okra, and salt, then steam again until fluffy.

steps.title: Cook garnish
steps.instruction: Fry onions, eggplant, and chilies in oil until soft and savory.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold garnish through the fonio or spoon it over the top.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Maru We Llham
recipes.description: Mauritanian meat, rice, and vegetable pot scented with mustard, onion, bay, pepper, and broth.
recipes.story: Maru we llham sits between Sahel rice pot and desert hospitality: meat, rice, vegetables, mustard sharpness, and a broth that feeds the whole tray.
recipes.occasion: Best for shared platters, family lunch, and one-pot rice meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Green salad, chili sauce, or Mauritanian mint tea after the meal.
recipes.regional_variations: Chicken, lamb, goat, or beef may be used; vegetables can include cassava, cabbage, carrot, or eggplant.
recipes.review_summary: Mauritanian references list Maru we llham as meat with rice and vegetables, and recipe sources include mustard sauce. The AfroKitchen version uses lamb or chicken with rice cooked in the broth.
recommended_changes: Brown meat before adding vegetables.
Use enough broth for the rice but avoid stirring once it starts steaming.

sources.title: Culinary Adventures with Cam - Maru We Llham

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Brown meat with onion and garlic in oil or butter.

steps.title: Braise
steps.instruction: Add mustard, bay, vegetables, salt, pepper, and stock. Simmer until meat is nearly tender.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rice, cover, and cook on low until grains absorb the broth.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Rest covered, then fluff gently and serve from a shared platter.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Dounguouri Soko
recipes.description: Nigerien white bean and lamb stew cooked with onion, tomato, pepper, garlic, and a slow savory sauce.
recipes.story: Dounguouri Soko is Niger's bean stew comfort, built like a Sahelian cousin to cassoulet: white beans, lamb, tomato, pepper, and time.
recipes.occasion: Best for cool evenings, bean lovers, and hearty family stews.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, millet, flatbread, or a spoon of mayonnaise as some references note.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use natron to soften beans; others skip it and simply soak beans overnight.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on white beans, lamb, onion, peppers, tomato, and long cooking. The AfroKitchen version keeps natron optional and relies on soaking.
recommended_changes: Soak beans overnight.
Keep tomato out until beans have softened.

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in fresh water until just tender.

steps.title: Brown lamb
steps.instruction: Brown lamb in oil, then add onions and peppers and cook until softened.

steps.title: Simmer stew
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, cooked beans, and stock. Simmer until lamb and beans are tender.

steps.title: Reduce
steps.instruction: Simmer uncovered to thicken, then adjust salt and pepper.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Kopto
recipes.description: Nigerien moringa leaf salad-sauce mixed with peanut paste, soumbala, onion, chile, and sometimes gari.
recipes.story: Kopto means leaf in Zarma, and this Nigerien dish lets moringa stay green and direct, dressed with peanut, soumbala, onion, and pepper.
recipes.occasion: Best for light lunches, side plates, and moringa season.
recipes.best_served_with: Dambou, rice, millet, gari, grilled fish, or beans.
recipes.regional_variations: Moringa is classic, but cabbage, spinach, or white bissap leaves may stand in when moringa is not available.
recipes.review_summary: Nigerien sources describe kopto as moringa leaves mixed with peanut paste, soumbala, chile, onion, and optional gari. The AfroKitchen version keeps cooking brief.
recommended_changes: Drain leaves well so the peanut dressing stays creamy.
Use soumbala lightly because it is powerful.

sources.title: Le Sahel - Mets traditionnels du Niger

steps.title: Blanch leaves
steps.instruction: Blanch moringa leaves briefly, then drain and squeeze out excess water.

steps.title: Make dressing
steps.instruction: Mix peanut paste, soumbala, chili, onion, salt, and a splash of water into a creamy dressing.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold leaves into the dressing until evenly coated.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Top with tomato, pepper, or gari if desired and serve cool or room temperature.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Binch Akara
recipes.description: Sierra Leonean black-eyed bean fritters, whipped light and fried until crisp, often sold with onion gravy or bread.
recipes.ethnic_group: Sierra Leonean Krio and West African
recipes.story: Binch akara is Sierra Leone's bean fritter language: peeled black-eyed peas, onion, pepper, air beaten into the batter, and a hot-oil finish.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, snacks, street-food boards, and party trays.
recipes.best_served_with: Onion gravy, bread rolls, pepper sauce, or pap.
recipes.regional_variations: Akara names travel across West Africa; Sierra Leonean vendors often serve binch akara as a street snack with onion gravy.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on black-eyed bean fritters and note Sierra Leonean street service. The AfroKitchen method emphasizes peeling and whipping for a lighter crumb.
recommended_changes: Peel beans well for a smooth batter.
Whip batter before frying so fritters puff.

steps.title: Peel beans
steps.instruction: Soak beans, rub off skins, and rinse until mostly clean.

steps.title: Blend batter
steps.instruction: Blend beans with onion, pepper, and minimal water into a thick paste.

steps.title: Whip and season
steps.instruction: Whip batter with salt and cayenne until lighter and slightly airy.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until golden and crisp.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Ablo
recipes.description: Togolese steamed rice-and-corn cakes, lightly sweet, soft, and served with stew, grilled fish, or pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Ablo is soft, white, and gently sweet, the steamed cake that makes pepper sauce, fish, or tomato stew feel complete.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, snacks, breakfast, and stew sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato stew, grilled fish, chicken, pepper sauce, or beans.
recipes.regional_variations: Togo, Benin, Ghana, and Ivory Coast all have related ablo or abolo styles using rice flour, corn flour, starch, yeast, and sugar.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on steamed rice/corn cakes with yeast and a mild sweetness. The AfroKitchen version ferments briefly for a practical home method.
recommended_changes: Let the batter rise until visibly bubbly.
Do not overfill molds because the cakes expand.

sources.title: African Food Network - Rice Cakes Ablo

steps.title: Mix batter
steps.instruction: Whisk flours, starch, sugar, yeast, salt, and warm water into a smooth pourable batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter rise until bubbly and slightly expanded.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Pour into greased molds and steam until set and springy.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Cool briefly before unmolding and serving with stew or pepper sauce.

recipes.official_source_status: No direct ministry recipe page was found for this dish in this batch pass. The recipe is synthesized from multiple culinary, cultural, and household-style references.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk
recipes.name: Djenkoume
recipes.description: Togolese tomato cornmeal dough cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and broth, often served with grilled chicken.
recipes.story: Djenkoume is tomato-seasoned cornmeal with backbone: toasted maize, onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and the patience to stir until it turns glossy.
recipes.occasion: Best for grilled chicken, fried fish, and Togolese party plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Togolese grilled chicken, fried fish, tomato sauce, or chili.
recipes.regional_variations: Related to Beninese amiwo, some versions use chicken broth, dried shrimp, coconut milk, or a separate tomato sauce.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree that Djenkoume is a Togolese tomato cornmeal staple. The AfroKitchen version toasts the cornmeal lightly and uses palm oil for color and flavor.
recommended_changes: Pour cornmeal slowly while stirring to avoid lumps.
Keep the heat medium-low once the dough thickens.

steps.title: Toast cornmeal
steps.instruction: Toast cornmeal in a dry pot, stirring, until it smells nutty but does not burn.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: In another pot, cook onion, garlic, ginger, tomatoes, tomato paste, and palm oil into a red sauce.

steps.title: Stir dough
steps.instruction: Add broth, then stream in cornmeal while stirring constantly until thick and smooth.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Cook until the dough pulls from the pot, then shape and serve hot.
